title: Greetings & Goodbyes
shortSummary: Greet people, introduce yourself, and say goodbye in Turkish, the foundation of every conversation.
culturalNote: Turks greet warmly and value courtesy. Merhaba is the safe, friendly hello for almost any situation, while Selam is more casual among friends and younger people. The biggest social choice is sen versus siz: use the informal sen with friends, family, and children, but use the formal, plural siz with strangers, elders, shopkeepers, and anyone older or in authority until you are invited to switch. Adding bey (for men) or hanım (for women) after a first name (Ahmet Bey, Ayşe Hanım) is a respectful default with people you do not know well. When leaving, the person staying says güle güle (go smiling) and the person going says hoşça kal (stay well), so the two farewells are not interchangeable. A handshake suits first meetings, while close friends and relatives often greet with a kiss on each cheek.

grammar.explanation: Turkish has two ways to say 'you': sen (informal singular) and siz (formal or plural). Use sen with friends, family, and children. Use siz with strangers, elders, and in shops or at work. The siz form also adds the -sınız ending to verbs and is the polite default until someone offers sen.
grammar.commonMistake: Using sen with someone you just met or who is older, which can sound rude or overly familiar.

grammar.rule: Two goodbyes: hoşça kal and güle güle
grammar.explanation: When parting, the person who is leaving says hoşça kal (stay well, informal) or hoşça kalın (formal/plural), and the person who is staying replies güle güle. They are a matched pair, not interchangeable, so you choose based on who is going and who is staying.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying güle güle when you are the one walking out the door; the person leaving says hoşça kal.

dialogue.setup: Ayşe Hanım meets her new colleague Mehmet Bey in the office in the morning.

lines.speaker: Mehmet Bey
lines.english: Good morning! I'm well, thank you. How are you?

lines.speaker: Ayşe Hanım
lines.native: Ben de iyiyim. Ben Ayşe, yeni proje müdürü.
lines.english: I'm well too. I'm Ayşe, the new project manager.

lines.speaker: Mehmet Bey
lines.english: Nice to meet you. I'm Mehmet.

lines.speaker: Ayşe Hanım
lines.native: Ben de memnun oldum. Ekibe hoş geldiniz!
lines.english: Nice to meet you too. Welcome to the team!

lines.speaker: Mehmet Bey
lines.english: Thank you very much. See you later.

lines.speaker: Ayşe Hanım
lines.english: See you, have a good day's work!

quiz.question: How do you say 'Good morning' in Turkish?
quiz.explanation: Günaydın is used from early morning until midday.

quiz.question: Which is the formal way to ask 'How are you?'
quiz.explanation: Nasılsınız? uses the polite siz form; the others are casual.

quiz.question: The person leaving says 'Hoşça _____' and the one staying replies 'güle güle'.
quiz.explanation: Hoşça kal (stay well) is said by the person who is leaving.

quiz.question: What does a person who is STAYING say when someone leaves?
quiz.explanation: Güle güle (go smiling) is the farewell said by the person staying behind.

quiz.question: Arrange: oldum / memnun
quiz.explanation: Memnun oldum means 'Nice to meet you', literally 'I became pleased'.
